findings.claim: In ptr_capture_formula the subscript/extraction ops (`[`, `[[`, `$`) are a SEPARATE branch from the string-builder ops (`paste`, `paste0`, `sprintf`, `c`, `vector`, …) precisely because a subscript may resolve to a language object (a quoted formula picked out of a list/env) while a string-builder must resolve to a string.
findings.detail: Before 2026-06-02 both groups shared ONE branch that accepted only a string and aborted on anything else ("got a language of length 3"), which is why `ptr_ui(plots[[1]])` with `plots` holding `expr(...)` failed and required `!!plots[[1]]`. The fix split them: the `[`/`[[`/`$` branch now forces the value in `envir` and accepts a string OR a language value — the language case is assigned to `formula_captured` and falls through to the shared wrapper-unwrap + `expr_text(width=500L)` deparse, mirroring the bare-symbol path higher up. String-builders stayed string-only on purpose (a `c(...)` of exprs is not a single formula). A non-string/non-language subscript value (a number, or a single-bracket `[` yielding a sub-list) is still rejected. All four public entry points (ptr_app/ptr_server/ptr_ui/ptr_ui_controls) funnel through this one function, so the bare-subscript capability is uniform across them.
findings.verification: On main@30fb935 the two branches are present and distinct: R/paintr-app.R:269 `else if (head_name %in% c("[", "[[", "$"))` (accepts string OR language, falls through to deparse) and R/paintr-app.R:293 `else if (head_name %in% c("paste", "paste0", ...))` (string-only). Stamp's line estimate ~L251-300 was approximate; actual L269/293.

findings.claim: The l2-shared / l2-shared-partition e2e fixtures already call `ptr_ui(plots[[1]], …)` bare, but their `plots` list holds STRING formulas, so that bare-subscript call always worked via the string branch — the e2e suite therefore does NOT exercise the expr-in-a-list subscript path that the 2026-06-02 fix added.
findings.detail: Consequence for coverage reasoning: a green l2-shared e2e proves the string-subscript path is intact, but the new language-subscript capability is grounded only by the unit/integration tests in test-ui-expression-input.R. The captured string for `plots[[1]]` (expr) is byte-identical to what `!!plots[[1]]` produces, and the `!!` path is e2e-covered, so runtime behavior is provably unchanged — but no browser test boots an expr-list directly.
findings.verification: l2-shared/app.R:36-39 `plots <- list("ggplot(iris, aes(x = ppMetric(shared = 'metric'), ...))", ...)` — both elements are character strings, not exprs. grep confirms test-e2e-vignette-examples-shinytest2.R is the only test booting the l2-shared fixture. So bare ptr_ui(plots[[1]]) there hits the string return; the new language path is unexercised by e2e.

findings.claim: Byte-for-byte HTML equality between two ptr_ui builds that contain a `shared = "..."` placeholder is order-dependent within one R session and is an unreliable test oracle; test formula-capture equivalence by calling ptr_capture_formula directly (hand-written deparse oracle) or compare bare-vs-`!!` on a shared-free formula instead.
findings.detail: EMPIRICALLY OBSERVED this session: across two ptr_ui(...shared=...) calls in one process, the inline `ptr-shared-panel` block appeared in one build and not the other depending on call order, diverging the HTML independent of the formula. The actionable lesson (don't use shared-panel HTML equality as an oracle) is solid. UNVERIFIED part: the mechanism ('process-global state') was inferred, not traced, and the stamp cited a non-existent file `R/paintr-ui.R`. The shared panel is actually rendered out of R/paintr-shared-ui.R / R/paintr-region.R / R/paintr-shared-coordinator.R (grep on main@30fb935). Follow-up to upgrade to verified: trace which env/counter the inline shared section keys off and confirm it is process-global vs build-ordinal.
findings.verification: Stamp's cited `R/paintr-ui.R` does not exist (no such file on main). Symptom (order-dependent shared-panel HTML) was reproduced live this session via Rscript probes, but the 'process-global state' mechanism was never traced to a specific symbol, so the causal claim is unconfirmed. Corrected the source to the real shared-panel files for the follow-up trace.

findings.claim: CORRECTION of the original stamp: the surprise `chore(knowledge)` commit (310a8c4) that appeared as the first parent of the feature merge was NOT a git-hook auto-commit of the dirty tree — it was a CONCURRENT session's deliberate human-authored commit that advanced main's tip between the pre-merge inspection and the merge.
findings.detail: Original stamp claimed a `--no-ff` merge into a dirty main triggered a hook to auto-commit the uncommitted .claude/harvest-findings/* + symbol-lines.json as 310a8c4. That mechanism is FALSE: `git show -s 310a8c4` shows author==committer==willju-wangqian <wju@iastate.edu> at 2026-06-02 07:29:07 with a deliberate message; dev/githooks/pre-commit only `git add`s (symbol-lines regen), neither pre-commit nor post-merge runs `git commit`. So a concurrent Claude session (the parallel-sessions hazard) committed the harvest work itself, moving main 29571ac→310a8c4, and the merge merely incorporated it as the first parent. Durable lesson (reframed): when working a feature in a worktree while another session works main, main's tip can advance under you; a surprising new commit in your merge's ancestry is likely the other session's, so inspect authorship before attributing it to tooling.
findings.source: git: 310a8c4 (author/committer willju-wangqian 2026-06-02T07:29:07), merge 30fb935 parents [310a8c4, 72227f9]; dev/githooks/pre-commit, dev/githooks/post-merge
findings.verification: git show -s --format='%an %ae %ai / %cn %ce %ci' 310a8c4 → both author and committer are willju-wangqian <wju@iastate.edu> at 2026-06-02 07:29:07 (a normal human commit, not a hook). grep 'git commit' in dev/githooks/ finds none; pre-commit only stages symbol-lines.json. Hence the original 'hook auto-committed during merge' causal claim is contradicted; the real cause is a concurrent session committing main.
